reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity NGO interest in access for high-ability low-SES pupils; documented commitment to reducing gatekeeping bias and information-asymmetry harms (e.g., Roma Education Fund publications on coach-driven selection; Opportunity for All / Esélyek Egyenlősége Coalition statements on teacher-level discretion as a vector of discrimination).
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils screened out of selective tracks; families without coaching access or cultural navigation capital; the 88–92% majority in non-selective schools who see no direct benefit.
reactions.public_good_frame: Reducing information-asymmetry capture of selective entry opens the gate to merit regardless of parental SES; system-wide equity improves only if the non-selected majority also gains, which S1 does not guarantee.
reactions.argument: S1 helps a narrow slice — high-ability poor pupils who would otherwise be screened out — and we defend that gain. Standardised central assessment and lottery-dampening reduce teacher-level gatekeeping and coaching arms race, both documented vectors of discrimination. HOWEVER: S1 leaves the track structure and peer-sorting intact, so the 88–92% majority in general schools — disproportionately low-SES and Roma — see no structural change. Offsetting gains for selective entrants against persistent segregation elsewhere is not equity; it is triage. We support S1 as a floor reform (stop the bleeding at the gate), but only paired with parallel investment in general schools or structural reduction of early sorting. S1 alone risks legitimising the system while micro-optimising access to it. The maintainer-exemption risk is severe: if church/foundation schools absorb selective demand when state intake is constrained, S1 simply re-sorts the problem into tax-exempt channels, worsening it.
reactions.condition_to_change: We move from conditional to support if: (1) S1 is paired with meaningful investment in the non-selected 88–92% (e.g., concurrent advanced programmes in general schools, or a staged cap on early-entry places); AND (2) maintainer-uniform admission rules are legislated as a precondition, not left to post-reform negotiation. We move to oppose if the ≥85% SES-classification audit is weakened below verifiable accuracy, converting the reform into a cosmetic gate-shuffling that harms low-SES entrants by excluding them under the pretence of standardisation.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented equity interest in delaying sorting to improve outcome distribution (e.g., Esélyek Egyenlősége Coalition position on tracking timing; OECD equity reviews citing later-tracking countries' advantage). The phase-down mechanism protects continuity via felmenő rendszer, which aligns with Roma and low-SES family interests in predictability and non-abrupt exclusion.
reactions.interest: The growing cohort exposed to mixed-ability lower-secondary over the decade; low-SES and Roma pupils in general schools who would remain under the status quo; rural and disadvantaged communities benefiting from reduced cream-skimming; families dependent on state education (who fear sudden selective expansion).
reactions.public_good_frame: Gradually widening the pool exposed to mixed-ability secondary is the strongest documented path to equity outcomes. The felmenő rendszer protects enrolled cohorts (a key equity safeguard, as abrupt exclusion disproportionately harms those already in the system). Sustained capacity reinvestment in general schools (step 5) is what the equity gain depends on — not just cap reduction, but actual strengthening of the alternative.
reactions.argument: S2 is the only scenario that restructures early sorting itself — the root mechanism. Declining caps move Hungary toward later-tracking systems with documented equity advantages (moderate evidence, cross-national). The felmenő rendszer is critical for us: it means no child already admitted is thrown out mid-track, which is an equity red line — abrupt exclusion would disproportionately harm those already invested in the system. The step-1 legislative lock (multi-party pact) is essential: without institutional teeth, a new government reverts caps overnight, wasting the decade. Poland 2016–2019 is the warning. We note that S2 ONLY works if the freed capacity is genuinely reinvested (step 5). If the government takes the savings instead, S2 becomes managed decline of state education, which harms the low-SES families who depend on it. The non-state migration risk is real: if selective demand migrates to church/foundation schools (step 4 mitigation), S2 re-sorts rather than reduces sorting. That mitigation must be binding — not aspirational.
reactions.condition_to_change: We move to oppose if: (1) the legislative lock is omitted or weakly framed, leaving the cap trajectory reversible; (2) freed capacity is absorbed as budget savings rather than reinvested in general-school programmes; (3) non-state maintainer caps are left unbound, allowing migration to neutralise the sorting reduction. We move to no_position on the advanced-programmes mechanism (step 5) because the equity NGO interest is in outcome, not in which within-school model achieves it — but we flag that 'retention of ambitious families' is an expert assumption, not an equity interest. If ambitious families leave, that is a middle-class political risk, not an equity failure.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Strong international evidence on delayed selection and equity outcomes (cited in scenario; e.g., OECD Equity and Quality in Education, 2012; Hanushek & Wößmann on tracking timing). Roma Education Fund and Esélyek Egyenlősége Coalition documented support for removal of early selection as a pathway to integration. The pedagogus_erdekvedo interest (teacher-union voice) is represented separately; we note the equity condition explicitly: teacher shortage is a real constraint, not a reason to accept early sorting.
reactions.interest: The entire cohort, reaching far beyond the selected slice; low-SES, Roma, rural, and first-generation pupils who are removed from mixed-ability exposure under early tracking; families who cannot navigate or afford selective-school logistics; the system-wide equity gain if peer effects are asymmetric and middle-class exit is contained.
reactions.public_good_frame: Delaying selection to age 14 is the strongest documented system-wide equity intervention. If peer effects are asymmetric (helping low-achieving pupils more than high-achieving pupils lose), the whole cohort gains, not a slice. This is equity as structural, not compensatory. It also addresses segregation within the state system, preventing cream-skimming that hollows rural networks.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that removes early selection itself — the sorting mechanism at its root. Cross-national evidence on delayed tracking is strong (moderate confidence in the scenario's framing). The load-bearing assumption is asymmetric peer effects: if peers are symmetric (zero-sum), S3 redistributes learning but does not create it, and may trigger middle-class flight that negates the gain. That is testable (step 2, pilot with independent evaluation). We are NOT assuming S3 is free: the costs are high (teacher retraining, school reconfiguration, diagnostic support). We are NOT assuming the political window is open: the scenario flags that middle-class resistance is real and can be reversed by electoral turnover (Poland precedent). We ARE insisting that teacher shortage is not a reason to accept selection — it is a reason to invest in teacher supply before rolling out S3 (step 1, the 10-year forecast precondition is non-negotiable for us). The equity-negative threshold (top-SES quartile state enrolment below two-thirds) is a safeguard we support: if exit past that point, the reform redistributes privilege rather than reducing it, and should pause. On choice and catchment (step 4): this is essential. Removing tracks without reforming school choice means high-SES families re-sort via neighbourhood and non-state channels, defeating the integration. We do NOT take a position on whether the teacher shortage is 'fixed' (scenario flags this as a contested political judgment); we take the position that rollout must be conditional on visible supply stabilisation, not assumed.
reactions.condition_to_change: We move to oppose if: (1) the pilot evaluation (step 2) shows peer effects are symmetric or harmful to high-achieving pupils (i.e., the asymmetry assumption fails), or if middle-class exit exceeds the equity-negative threshold and the government does not pause; (2) the teacher-supply forecast (step 1) is absent or shows no stabilisation path, and rollout proceeds anyway; (3) choice and catchment rules are not reformed concurrently, meaning high-SES families re-sort through non-state channels and the integration fails. We move to conditional support if the teacher shortage is real but manageable — in that case, S3 rolls out sequentially (shortage-worst schools last, with proven retraining throughput), not nationally at once. The non-state binding caps (step 5) are essential: without them, S3 becomes an inadvertent subsidy to private education.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Equity interest in structural reduction of early sorting, documented in Roma Education Fund and Esélyek Egyenlősége Coalition statements on segregation as a root cause, not an effect to be compensated. The premise that 'structure is politically fixed' is contested: it is a political judgment, not evidence (scenario flags this explicitly).
reactions.interest: The 88–92% majority in non-selective schools who remain sorted; low-SES, Roma, rural, and first-generation pupils in segregated cohorts; families dependent on state education who need structural change, not permanent subsidy for inequality; the principle that funding alone does not undo segregation without structural integration.
reactions.public_good_frame: Segregation by sorting is not an outcome that can be 'offset' by investment alone — it is a mechanism that produces unequal outcomes regardless of resource level. Compensation is a floor (stop the learning loss), but leaving the sorting mechanism in place leaves the segregation mechanism in place. Equity is structural, not charitable.
reactions.argument: S4 treats early sorting as politically immovable and therefore works around it via targeted funding and curriculum standards. We oppose this frame, not the interventions. Targeted funding and curriculum raises are necessary (we support them), but they are NOT sufficient to undo the segregation effect of early selection. Portugal's TEIP success is moderate evidence for non-structural gains, but it is gained under LATER selection (age 14+, not 10/12). At Hungary's selection age, no country evidence exists that compensation offsets rather than merely masks segregation — S4 acknowledges this (unverifiable ex ante assumption). We will not build an equity strategy on an unverifiable assumption. S4 also risks legitimising the selective structure: by showing that general schools CAN perform well with investment, it may reduce political pressure to remove selection, inadvertently stabilising the very sorting it aims to compensate. The fiscal sensitivity (−30% budget) is a killer: targeted funding is the first cut in downturns, so S4 makes equity permanently fragile and reversible. Most importantly: S4 forecloses structural options by treating them as politically impossible. That political judgment may be wrong. If it is, S4 has wasted a decade defending a sorted system when the window to change it existed. We do NOT oppose investment in general schools — it is necessary in S1, S2, and S3 as well. We oppose the premise that early sorting must be accepted as fixed.
reactions.condition_to_change: We move to conditional support if: (1) S4 is explicitly framed as a FLOOR strategy to prevent further inequality while structural options (S2/S3) are pursued in parallel, NOT as an alternative to them; AND (2) the annual evaluation (step 5) uses the offset-vs-mask distinction explicitly and shows evidence that compensation is offsetting segregation, not just raising average outcomes; AND (3) targeted funding is ring-fenced against budget cuts with a legal floor clause (not just a multi-year commitment), because −30% sensitivity is unacceptable. We move to oppose more firmly if: (1) S4 is presented as the only option because structure is 'politically fixed', without testing that premise; (2) no parallel structural exploration happens; or (3) the evaluation lacks the offset-vs-mask test, defaulting to the weaker assumption. We also note: if the premise that structure is politically fixed is wrong, S4 becomes a tragic foreclosure of a real equity window.
